dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a diabetic problem at 13th and Main, patient reported to have altered mental status noting confusion and disorientation.
Hello, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She has changed mental status-it started during dialysis and got us worried about her confusion and disorientation.
Ms. --, can you tell me your name?
Jennifer.
What month is it today, Jennifer?
Hmm...I think October? Not exactly sure.
I need you to squeeze my fingers with your hands, if you can.
squeezes fingers*.
Pupils equal and reactive bilaterally, approximately 3mm noted from visual inspection.
Do you have any trouble breathing or choking right now, Ms. Jennifer?
No.
We noticed your breathing is at 24 per minute. You mention it's normal for you, right?
Yes, that's my normal breathing rate.
Your airway is self-maintained. Please stay in a seated position for now to be safe.
Breath sounds are clear bilaterally. We're monitoring for any subtle signs of labored breathing or adventitious sounds.
Would you mind if I look at your chest for a moment, to check for any asymmetries or irregularities?
Okay.
No indication of asymmetric chest movement. Clear bilateral breath sounds observed during auscultation and visual inspection.
Oxygen saturation is 100 percent via finger pulse oximeter, consistent with admission readings in the ER.
Ready to check your blood pressure now to continue assessments.
Blood pressure is 138 over 70 mmHg, consistent with initial findings from the scene.
Pulse rate is 86 per minute, and it's regular and strong with good peripheral perfusion noted.
Skin condition appears warm and dry, and responsive during inspection for capillary refill time.
EKG shows sinus rhythm at current moment, no arrhythmias or ST changes recorded at this time.
Is there any pain or shortness of breath you'd like us to know about now, Jennifer?
No pain or shortness of breath to my knowledge.
Do you have any known drug allergies, or has staff noted anything in your file during your care here?
Patient has no documented drug allergies in file and dialysis staff observed no prior adverse reactions.
Can you tell me which medications you're currently on, or would dialysis staff be able to confirm that for us?
Jennifer is on Lantus, Humalog, simvastatin, amlodipine, bupropion, furosemide, and other dialysis-related medications such as multivitamins and heparin.
Additional medications noted are simvastatin (Vytorin), PEG 3350 (Gavilyte), and sevelamer (RenaGel) specifically for renal use according to staff.
What were you told about any existing medical conditions or chronic illnesses, Jennifer?
She has insulin-dependent diabetes, high cholesterol, and a history of high blood pressure.
When was the last time you ate or drank anything, Jennifer?
She was already nourished before the dialysis started and they haven't kept her NPO at any point noted here.
When exactly did this mental change first become noticeable, per your staff or from the patient?
She was undergoing dialysis when she was noted to become disoriented and less alert-change happened after about 2 hours and 15 minutes of treatment.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate any pain or discomfort right now, Jennifer?
0. I'm not in pain at all.
I'll need to briefly check your skin for rashes, bruising, or signs of infection-could you roll your sleeve up just a bit?
Skin is uniformly warm and dry with no signs of rashes, breaks, or unusual lesions observed.
May I inspect your eyes to evaluate your pupils and visual response to light, Ms. Jennifer?
Bilateral pupils equal and reactive with no evidence of extraocular muscle dysfunction or ptosis.
Checking neck veins-no distension noted and trachea is central. No signs of pain or tenderness on palpation.
No chest wall deformities observed. Proceeding with bilateral lung sound assessment.
Let me quickly listen to your lungs, okay? Please try to breathe normally for me.
Breath sounds are equal bilaterally, with no signs of wheezes, crackles, or bronchial sounds observed.
I'll need to do a quick abdominal exam. It's just to check for any tenderness or discomfort-would that be okay with you?
Go ahead.
The abdomen is soft upon palpation with good compliance, no tenderness, distension, or palpable masses detected.
Let me check your arms and legs-could you point to your hands or feet as I move them around?
No evidence of focal weakness, numbness, or motor deficit. Peripheral pulses are present and symmetrically strong with no prior vascular compromise reported.
Pelvis appears to be stable with no evidence of crepitus, bony deformities, or ligament instability from visual or palpation assessment.
Let me check your glucose level now-your blood sugar is at 600 mg/dL according to reading from the scene device.
Rechecking your blood pressure-your current reading is 153 over 77 mmHg.
Heart rate is 88 per minute and rhythm has remained consistent and regular thus far.
Respiratory rate remains at 24 per minute and is regular and effortless in appearance.
Oxygen saturation has stayed steady at 100 percent on our initial sweep.
Has Jennifer ever had any history of tardive dyskinesia, based on prior records?
Yes, she was previously diagnosed with tardive dyskinesia, but it's been under some management with support of her neurologist.
Based on findings and protocol 3-15 for medical - altered mental status, we'll transport under the category of hyperglycemia and AMS for ER management.
Preparing to place the patient on a 4-lead noninvasive cardiac monitor per standing protocol.
EKG readings are stable, showing a normal sinus rhythm with no acute changes observed for current encounter.
Moving now to collect a 12-lead EKG as part of the workup for further differential considerations.
The 12-lead EKG indicates normal sinus rhythm for a woman over 40 with no ST changes or arrhythmias present.
IV access was not attempted-dialysis staff reported poor vascular access suitable for needle placement.
We are doing a follow-up vitals check and blood pressure is now reported as 146 over 76 mmHg on recheck.
Heart rate decreased slightly to 87 but still normal for background condition. Rhythm remains in sinus.
Oxygen saturation is unchanged, remaining at 100 percent.
Glucose not statically rechecked-last assessment indicates a high level is likely based on prior status.
We are now preparing to transport you to Chippenham Medical Center as the closest appropriate facility for this presentation.
Okay.
Please assist her gently with transfer to the stretcher and ensure we avoid a fall given her AMS.
Receiving transfer to stretcher, will support weight shared between staff and our crew.
She will be placed in a semi-Fowler's position to optimize comfort and avoid respiratory compromise.
Five-point securing now complete with straps and shoulder restraints. Position stable.
We are beginning transport now with lights on for hospital readiness.
Jennifer, are there any other details from your history or condition you think the ER should know when we arrive?
No further symptoms just now. I'm not experiencing anything else outside of confusion.
Your reported pain is stable and still at zero. Is there anything else making you uncomfortable now?
Still okay-no pain.
Transport stable and within protocol. All systems go for hospital arrival.
Patient secured on board. Ready for transfer when the hospital is prepared for patient intake.
Arrived at the hospital with patient under escort-no critical changes from transport.
Beginning transfer of patient from ambulance to the ER stretcher for drip and further medical engagement.
Effectively transferred via draw sheet lateral pull for safe trauma-free move to the bed.
Extra precaution in keeping installed seat belts in place during hospital couch transfer.
Final report in hand to ER-51-year-old female with AMS and hyperglycemia, on dialysis, presenting from scene casualty transport.
Records of previous vital signs, medications, and past history provided for ER documentation review.
Patient currently unable to sign due to AMS-hospital staff noted high cognitive load at presentation point.
Delay expected at hospital's request due to current facility turnover for alternate patient priorities.
Final report initiated and verbally transferred via face-to-face handover to nursing team accepting patient care.
Hospital documentary process completed-full in-hand report and planned disposition are with the emergency care team.
EMS unit now available for next assignment according to dispatch parameters issued.
Transportation operation fully documented, patient in-stable condition pending further ER assessment.
Patient status is continued to best of knowledge, consistent with pre-hospital findings during EMS initial response and result tracking.
